title: Function reference
text: PdDataSet

title: Function reference
text: PdDataSet

title: Current features of pyLikeSAS
text:
Basic pyLikeSAS object PdDataSet extends pandas DataFrame class by ability to add/set following attributes (stored in the pandas DataFrame’s _attrs attribute):

the labels for columns (variables)
the formats for columns (variables)
to use SAS-like dataset options like:

the dataset label
drop/keep variables
rename variables
define sort by order
run SQL select on data set (or on more tables)


Concept of column formats in pyLikeSAS is covered by python dictionaries or mapping functions (see provided lookup_format function that covers also ranges of values along with missing and unknown values for particular variable of PdDataSet).
Note:
As PdDataSet class just extends the pandas DataFrame all capabilities of the pandas DataFrame are still available.



pyLikeSAS also includes function data_step that allows to mimic the behavior of SAS data step. The function is designed to be used with PdDataSet objects and allows to define the data step logic as a single python function. Return values from data step logic function are used to create the output PdDataSet object.
Following features of data step are supported:

define retain variables
define by groups (including first/last flag variables)
define output columns and their order in the output dataset
pass additional parameters to data step logic function (like dictionaries, lists, etc.)




PyODS class is a version of SAS Output Delivery System (ODS) in pyLikeSAS package. It aims to simple generation of outputs in various file formats (destinations) with color-full styles selected or defined by user. It includes:

support for titles/footnotes
outputs tables to the following formats:

text files
Jupyter notebooks
terminal (with formatting using tabulate package)
HTML files
Markdown
Latex

outputs figures (graphs) to the following file formats:

png,jpeg
SVG
PDF

outputs graphs as links or in place content to the output files
definition of own styles and templates
tracing of PyODS operation




No way to get even small part of all SAS procedures. Just following basic presentation functions are now integrated with PyODS class:

proc_print: print table
proc_figure: render graph from the python’s matplotlib figure

along with a few functions having (very limited) functionality of corresponding SAS procedures:

proc freq1: one dimensional frequency table(s)
proc freq2: two dimensional frequency table(s)
proc_contents: list contents of table - variables and their basic statistics
… more to come




There are additional tools included in pyLikeSAS:

PLS_logging - logging class for pyLikeSAS
diagnostics/helper functions

csv_info: get info about csv file
sas_sample_datasets: download data from SAS sample site
python environment info

… more to come

Following CSV file class.csv is used for demonstration of pyLikeSAS functionalities in all examples:

Content of CSV file class.csv is equal to the famous SASHELP.CLASS sample table. See module pyLikeSAS.sas_sample_datasets.py if you need to download it. After that CSV file is read into pd.DataFrame using following code:
# import core of pyLikeSAS 
import pandas as pd
from pyLikeSAS.PdDataSet import PdDataSet
from pyLikeSAS.lookup_format import lookup_format
from pyLikeSAS.proc_print import import proc_print
from pyLikeSAS.data_step import data_step

# read class.csv into pandas DataFrame
df = pd.read_csv(
    "./data/class.csv",
    dtype={'Weight':  float, 'Height':  float, }
)

# import core of pyLikeSAS 
import pandas as pd
from pyLikeSAS.PdDataSet import PdDataSet
from pyLikeSAS.lookup_format import lookup_format
from pyLikeSAS.proc_print import import proc_print
from pyLikeSAS.data_step import data_step

# read class.csv into pandas DataFrame
df = pd.read_csv(
    "./data/class.csv",
    dtype={'Weight':  float, 'Height':  float, }
)

title: Running Data Step with PdDataSet
text:
Ultimate goal of SAS data processing (apart from SQLs nad PROCs) is running data step. Data step is basically sequential processing of (rows of) input dataset during which variables (columns) are modified/appended with complex logic frequently based on the following data step rules:

every input row is processed independently (variables are read from input table). Order in which input rows are processed is defined by by parameter - that lists ordering variables.
the new derived variables could be defined in data step - but these has to be set within processing of one row in data step. Their state is reset for the next row of input.
only variables that keep their state during whole iteration over input rows are retained variables (variables defined only within data step - not read from input dataset). Retain variables should be defined as such and their initial value is usually set at the start of processing.
There is special (automatic) variable _n_ that is order of input row that is currently processed.
Similar to the (automatic) variable _n_ are (automatic) flag variables first._order_variable_ (and last._order_variable_) that indicates that particular ordering variable has the next value in the groups in sorted input rows (or has the last value in the groups). These variables allow to define complex group by processing only partially available in SQL window function.
row is (conditionally) written to output that could consist from subset of input variables/derived/retained variables.

Data step programming is very useful concept - when fully mastered it is hard to beat its simplicity,clarity and debugging enablement (when compared to SQL or DataFrame frameworks). Its implementation in pyLikeSAS is based on definition of python function that represent data step logic. That function is set as functional parameter of pyLikeSAS.data_step() function - that allows to define input/output data sets (and their dataset options), required ordering of input data set, retained variables, etc.
There should be two values returned by data step logic function, one is True/False flag indications that processed output row should be preserved/dropped. Current implementation supports just one output data set (and one input dataset)

that could be overcome by using sql parameter in the data set options of input data set.

(this is currently weakest point of pyLikeSAS - should be changed to allow more output rows from one call of data step logic function).



Simplest example follows (note use of data set option on PdDataSet objects):
def simple_BMI(
        # here we list all input variables (read from input data set) 
        Name,  Age,  Height, Weight,
        # here we are adding retain variables (with initial values)
        ith_14plus,
        # here are all automatic variables (to be optionally in data step logic)
        _n_
):
    #    define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
    #   + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
    # https://www.calculatorsoup.com/calculators/health/bmi-calculator.php


    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    # here we set value  new (potentially) output variables 
    # (defined in data step  logic)    
    # record input order into new variable
    nOBS = _n_+1  #SAS style nobs...
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2
    # only cases older than 14 years are kept in output dataset
    if Age>14:
        # usage of retain variable
        ith_14plus += 1
        return True,locals()
    else:
        return 0, locals()

# read input df
df = PdDataSet(pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv").head(18))
df

dfout = data_step(
    df.ds_options(drop='sex'),
    data_step_code_function=simple_BMI,
    # keep list of variables to be passed to output dataset
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Name', 'Age',    'BMI','ith_14plus'
    ],
    retain={'ith_14plus':0},
    format={ 'ith_14plus': "{:.1f}"}
)

proc_print(dfout.ds_options(format={'BMI': "{:.1f}"}),template='seaside')
which of course generates output data set similar to SQL result shown in example of data set options. Its additional variable ith_14plus would be available in SQL example only after using some window functions (or aggregations).






 


nOBS


Name


Age


BMI


ith_14plus

Second - more complex - data step example demonstrates usage of following features of data step (implementation in pyLikeSAS):

by processing including first/last flag variables
usage of retain variables
using native python object in the data step logic
usage of keep/label for output variables

"""
    test of pyLikeSAS.data_step using BMI index calculation
    and other bag of SAS tricks
"""


# function that converts BMI to message 
def bmi_label(value):
    # Function to categorize weight :
    if value < 18.5:
        return "Underweight"
    elif 18.5 <= value < 25.0:
        return "Normal"
    elif 25.0 <= value < 40:
        return "Overweight"
    else:
        return "Obese"


def inch_pounds(
        # data step automatic variables
        _n_, first, last, *,
        # input data set variables + retain variables
        Name,  Age, Sex, Height, Weight, sumBMI, BMI, nOBS
):
    """
        define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
        + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
         _n_, first, last, * are comnpulsory "SAS automatic" variables
        the rest of var are
            -  columns of the input dataframe (in any order)
            - retain variables  (see retain parameter of data_step function)
    """
    nOBS = _n_+1  # SAS style nobs...
    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2
    msg = bmi_label(BMI)
    # skip row with Mary
    if Name == 'Mary':
        return 0, locals()

    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')
    if Surname == 'n/a':
        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)
    if Sex == 'M':
        Name = 'Mr. '+Name
    else:
        Name = 'Miss '+Name

    #  first/last processing: note nice  SAS syntax and their immutability
    # print(type(first.Sex))
    if first.Sex:
        sumBMI = 0.
    sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI
    first_Sex = first.Sex
    # print(type(first_Sex))
    first_Age = first.Age
    last_Sex = last.Sex
    last_Age = last.Age
    return 1, locals()


# read input df
df = pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv")
df

# define objects to be used pythonically in data step logic inch_pounds
surname_dict = {'Alice': 'Caroll', 'James': 'Joyce'}

dfout = data_step(
    df,
    data_step_code_function=inch_pounds,
    retain={'BMI': 0, 'sumBMI': 0, 'nOBS': 0},
    # output dataframe columns order is defined by order in function or by keep
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Sex', 'Age',  'Name', 'Surname',  'BMI', 'sumBMI',
        'first_Sex', 'first_Age', 'last_Sex', 'last_Age'
    ],
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"},
    # or use drop=['Sex'],
    by=['Sex', 'Age'],
    by_order=[True, False]
)
proc_print(dfout)
Output dataset contains BMI, cumulative sum of BMI (over gender), first/last flag variables, all sorted by sex and (descending) age.

Data step also generated dictionary of updated surnames:
dfout2 = pd.DataFrame(
    data=[tuple(surname_dict), surname_dict.values()]
).transpose().set_axis(['Name', 'Surname'], axis=1)
display(dfout2)

Above pyLikeSAS data step example could be written in SAS as follows:
/* SAS code for BMI */

proc format;
      value bmi_label
      /*    # Function to categorize weight :*/
      low - 18.5 = "Underweight"
      18.5 < - 25.0 = "Normal"
      25.0 < - 40 = "Overweight"
      other = "Obese"
;
run;


proc sort data = sashelp.class out = class;
      by sex descending age;
run;

data dfout;
      length name $50;
      retain BMI 0 sumBMI 0 nOBS;

      set class (obs=19 rename=(name=name_ori));
      by sex descending age;

      drop name_ori;

      nOBS = _n_;
      Height = Height*2.54/100;
      Weight = Weight*0.45359237;
      BMI = Weight/(Height)**2;

      msg = put(BMI,bmi_label.);

      /*     skip row with Mary*/
      if name_ori='Mary' then do;
            return;
      end;

      /*    surname dictionary could be implemented using hash...*/
      /*    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')*/
      /*    if Surname == 'n/a':*/
      /*        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)*/
      if Sex = 'M' then
          Name = 'Mr. ' || name_ori;
      else
          Name = 'Miss ' || name_ori;

      if first.Sex then do;
          sumBMI = 0.;
      end;
      sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI;
      first_Sex = first.Sex;
      first_Age = first.Age;
      last_Sex = last.Sex;
      last_Age = last.Age;
      output;
run;
proc print;run;
SAS code is remarkably similar - just update of surname dictionary was not implemented (could be done using SAS hash object).

title: Running Data Step with PdDataSet
text:
Ultimate goal of SAS data processing (apart from SQLs nad PROCs) is running data step. Data step is basically sequential processing of (rows of) input dataset during which variables (columns) are modified/appended with complex logic frequently based on the following data step rules:

every input row is processed independently (variables are read from input table). Order in which input rows are processed is defined by by parameter - that lists ordering variables.
the new derived variables could be defined in data step - but these has to be set within processing of one row in data step. Their state is reset for the next row of input.
only variables that keep their state during whole iteration over input rows are retained variables (variables defined only within data step - not read from input dataset). Retain variables should be defined as such and their initial value is usually set at the start of processing.
There is special (automatic) variable _n_ that is order of input row that is currently processed.
Similar to the (automatic) variable _n_ are (automatic) flag variables first._order_variable_ (and last._order_variable_) that indicates that particular ordering variable has the next value in the groups in sorted input rows (or has the last value in the groups). These variables allow to define complex group by processing only partially available in SQL window function.
row is (conditionally) written to output that could consist from subset of input variables/derived/retained variables.

Data step programming is very useful concept - when fully mastered it is hard to beat its simplicity,clarity and debugging enablement (when compared to SQL or DataFrame frameworks). Its implementation in pyLikeSAS is based on definition of python function that represent data step logic. That function is set as functional parameter of pyLikeSAS.data_step() function - that allows to define input/output data sets (and their dataset options), required ordering of input data set, retained variables, etc.
There should be two values returned by data step logic function, one is True/False flag indications that processed output row should be preserved/dropped. Current implementation supports just one output data set (and one input dataset)

that could be overcome by using sql parameter in the data set options of input data set.

(this is currently weakest point of pyLikeSAS - should be changed to allow more output rows from one call of data step logic function).

title: Running Data Step with PdDataSet
text:
Simplest example follows (note use of data set option on PdDataSet objects):
def simple_BMI(
        # here we list all input variables (read from input data set) 
        Name,  Age,  Height, Weight,
        # here we are adding retain variables (with initial values)
        ith_14plus,
        # here are all automatic variables (to be optionally in data step logic)
        _n_
):
    #    define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
    #   + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
    # https://www.calculatorsoup.com/calculators/health/bmi-calculator.php


    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    # here we set value  new (potentially) output variables 
    # (defined in data step  logic)    
    # record input order into new variable
    nOBS = _n_+1  #SAS style nobs...
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2

        # usage of retain variable
        ith_14plus += 1
        return True,locals()
    else:
        return 0, locals()

# read input df
df = PdDataSet(pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv").head(18))
df

dfout = data_step(
    df.ds_options(drop='sex'),
    data_step_code_function=simple_BMI,
    # keep list of variables to be passed to output dataset
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Name', 'Age',    'BMI','ith_14plus'
    ],
    retain={'ith_14plus':0},
    format={ 'ith_14plus': "{:.1f}"}
)

proc_print(dfout.ds_options(format={'BMI': "{:.1f}"}),template='seaside')
which of course generates output data set similar to SQL result shown in example of data set options. Its additional variable ith_14plus would be available in SQL example only after using some window functions (or aggregations).

title: Running Data Step with PdDataSet
text:
Second - more complex - data step example demonstrates usage of following features of data step (implementation in pyLikeSAS):

by processing including first/last flag variables
usage of retain variables
using native python object in the data step logic
usage of keep/label for output variables

"""
    test of pyLikeSAS.data_step using BMI index calculation
    and other bag of SAS tricks
"""


# function that converts BMI to message 
def bmi_label(value):
    # Function to categorize weight :
    if value < 18.5:
        return "Underweight"
    elif 18.5 <= value < 25.0:
        return "Normal"
    elif 25.0 <= value < 40:
        return "Overweight"
    else:
        return "Obese"


def inch_pounds(
        # data step automatic variables
        _n_, first, last, *,
        # input data set variables + retain variables
        Name,  Age, Sex, Height, Weight, sumBMI, BMI, nOBS
):
    """
        define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
        + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
         _n_, first, last, * are comnpulsory "SAS automatic" variables
        the rest of var are
            -  columns of the input dataframe (in any order)
            - retain variables  (see retain parameter of data_step function)
    """
    nOBS = _n_+1  # SAS style nobs...
    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2
    msg = bmi_label(BMI)
    # skip row with Mary
    if Name == 'Mary':
        return 0, locals()

    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')
    if Surname == 'n/a':
        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)
    if Sex == 'M':
        Name = 'Mr. '+Name
    else:
        Name = 'Miss '+Name

    #  first/last processing: note nice  SAS syntax and their immutability
    # print(type(first.Sex))
    if first.Sex:
        sumBMI = 0.
    sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI
    first_Sex = first.Sex
    # print(type(first_Sex))
    first_Age = first.Age
    last_Sex = last.Sex
    last_Age = last.Age
    return 1, locals()


# read input df
df = pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv")
df

# define objects to be used pythonically in data step logic inch_pounds
surname_dict = {'Alice': 'Caroll', 'James': 'Joyce'}

dfout = data_step(
    df,
    data_step_code_function=inch_pounds,
    retain={'BMI': 0, 'sumBMI': 0, 'nOBS': 0},
    # output dataframe columns order is defined by order in function or by keep
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Sex', 'Age',  'Name', 'Surname',  'BMI', 'sumBMI',
        'first_Sex', 'first_Age', 'last_Sex', 'last_Age'
    ],
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"},
    # or use drop=['Sex'],
    by=['Sex', 'Age'],
    by_order=[True, False]
)
proc_print(dfout)
Output dataset contains BMI, cumulative sum of BMI (over gender), first/last flag variables, all sorted by sex and (descending) age.

Above pyLikeSAS data step example could be written in SAS as follows:
/* SAS code for BMI */

proc format;
      value bmi_label
      /*    # Function to categorize weight :*/
      low - 18.5 = "Underweight"
      18.5 < - 25.0 = "Normal"
      25.0 < - 40 = "Overweight"
      other = "Obese"
;
run;


proc sort data = sashelp.class out = class;
      by sex descending age;
run;

data dfout;
      length name $50;
      retain BMI 0 sumBMI 0 nOBS;

      set class (obs=19 rename=(name=name_ori));
      by sex descending age;

      drop name_ori;

      nOBS = _n_;
      Height = Height*2.54/100;
      Weight = Weight*0.45359237;
      BMI = Weight/(Height)**2;

      msg = put(BMI,bmi_label.);

      /*     skip row with Mary*/
      if name_ori='Mary' then do;
            return;
      end;

      /*    surname dictionary could be implemented using hash...*/
      /*    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')*/
      /*    if Surname == 'n/a':*/
      /*        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)*/
      if Sex = 'M' then
          Name = 'Mr. ' || name_ori;
      else
          Name = 'Miss ' || name_ori;

      if first.Sex then do;
          sumBMI = 0.;
      end;
      sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI;
      first_Sex = first.Sex;
      first_Age = first.Age;
      last_Sex = last.Sex;
      last_Age = last.Age;
      output;
run;
proc print;run;
SAS code is remarkably similar - just update of surname dictionary was not implemented (could be done using SAS hash object).

title: Purpose
text:
pyLikeSAS is my toy project. It started as part of my python learning and development path (back in Covid-19 years, followed by years of sporadic progress in my python adoption).
And now … yes, currently implemented pyLikeSAS is pythonic version of SAS-like dataset, data step, ODS and a few simple procedures.
So a tiny version of my dreamed involvement in development of the original SAS System did happened - just in python v3.12 instead of Lattice C. I did tap into the vast ecosystem of python open source modules, so starting in the way different place than a standard C libraries. Also I just followed SAS functionality and not invented all that old great SAS stuff. I did not need to care about performance and customers - it was all about my own learning and fun.
Now it seems, pyLikeSAS turned into learning resource aimed to be consumed by two distinct groups. pyLikeSAS could help to:

improve python skills of hard core SAS users (as the real programer can write Fortran code in any language ) and
demonstrate strengths of SAS to pythonic cake-eaters https://pbm.com/~lindahl/real.programmers.html

Hopefully, it enables both these groups to jump into projects coded in python while being confident that couple of things so well working in SAS will be fully in their hands and feel safely isolated from the ever changing python and its modules versions.
My 20+ years experience with SAS workloads, that basically read/update data and generate outputs in various formats while logging detail log file was not easily replicated in my beginner python. It was the main motivation for PyODS and PLSlogger. In additions such python workloads are developed in Jupyter and later scheduled to be run in batch (i.e. like in terminal). So pyLikeSAS package is designed to be used in Jupyter notebooks or as the plain python sources with automatic switching of outputs. And of course SAS data step was really missing in the pandas world.
In any case pyLikeSAS package is not intended to be used in the production code. That is why I am placing here the disclaimer (that I had been using in the old times even on the paid customer projects):
This software is not designed or intended for use in on-line control of aircraft, air traffic, aircraft navigation or aircraft communications or in the design, construction, operation or maintenance of any nuclear facility.




implement SAS data set like formats and labels on columns of the DataFrame
implement SAS data set options on top of DataFrame
implement function that will process DataFrame like SAS data step
implement capability to generate various outputs (formats/styles) in SAS ODS like fashion
gradually implement couple of useful SAS PROCs as python functions
having fun all the way along





performance, could be improved (or even tested)…
support for date/time columns (in simple, deficient, but elegant SAS manner)
more input/output data sets in the data step
definition of user styles in PyODS
PyODS prints in python dict format (prettyprint not json)
support of graphical outputs using terminalplot package
any kind of rtf format support
many more items still not spotted yet …

text:
pyLikeSAS is my toy project. It started as part of my python learning and development path (back in Covid-19 years, followed by years of sporadic progress in my python adoption).
And now … yes, currently implemented pyLikeSAS is pythonic version of SAS-like dataset, data step, ODS and a few simple procedures.
So a tiny version of my dreamed involvement in development of the original SAS System did happened - just in python v3.12 instead of Lattice C. Also I just followed SAS functionality and not invented all that old great SAS stuff. I did not need to care about performance and customers - it was all about my own learning and fun.
Now it seems, pyLikeSAS turned into learning resource aimed to be consumed by two distinct groups. pyLikeSAS could help to:

Hopefully, it enables both these groups to jump into projects coded in python while being confident that couple of things so well working in SAS will be fully in their hands and feel safely isolated from the ever changing python and its modules versions.
My 20+ years experience with SAS workloads, that basically read/update data and generate outputs in various formats while logging detail log file was not easily replicated in my beginner python. title: PdDataSet
text:
PdDataSet





Name
Description




PdDataSet
PdDataSet Class - implementation of SASlike data set including dataset options on top of pandas DataFrame






PdDataSet.PdDataSet(data, *args, desc=None, label=None, format=None, **kwargs)
PdDataSet Class - implementation of SASlike data set including dataset options on top of pandas DataFrame





Name
Description




ds_options
method that implements SAS like dataset options for PdDataSet object.





PdDataSet.PdDataSet.ds_options(
    obs=None,
    firstobs=None,
    where=None,
    sql=None,
    keep=None,
    drop=None,
    rename=None,
    label=None,
    format=None,
    desc=None,
    sqlengine='duck',
    ducktbls=None,
    ducktbls_aliases=None,
)
method that implements SAS like dataset options for PdDataSet object.
there is an example::











Name
Type
Description
Default




obs
int | tuple | list[tuple] | None
Defines selection of observations (rows). Examples of obs parameter usage: - Pass tuple with from-to values for example:obs=(0,4) - or list of from-to tuples, for example:obs=[(1,5),(12,-1),...] - Use (n,None) for reading from n-th row to the last row.
None


firstobs
int | None
Sets first observation to read, obs then should be int and represents last observation.
None


where
str | None
SQL where clause to be applied data set.
None


sql
str | None
SQL select statement be applied. Use self as table name for the parent DataFrame or use other DataFrames as names of the other tables.
None


keep
str | list[str] | None
list of columns to keep.
None


drop
str | list[str] | None
list of columns to drop.
None


rename
dict[str, str] | None
Dictionary that defines renaming of column names Example:: rename= { ‘oriname1’:‘newname1’, ‘oriname2’:‘newname2’,… ‘orinameN’:‘newnameN’ }
None


label
dict[str, str] | None
Dictionary that sets labelsof columns to be used when printed Example:: label= { ‘colname1’:‘long label for colname1’, ‘colname2’:‘longest label for colname2’,… }
None


format
dict[str, str | Callable] | None
Dictionary thatsets formats of columns to be used when printed Example:: format= { ‘colname1’:formatting_function, ‘colname2’:‘{:,.0f}’,… } formatting_function is a callable function that takes a value and returns a formatted string.
None


desc
str | None
sets descriptive label of dataset.
None


sqlengine
str
name of SQL engine duckdb or pandasql (pandasql is used only if not set to default duckdb)
'duck'


ducktbls
list[pd.DataFrame] | None
List of DataFrames to be passed to duckdb
None


ducktbls_aliases
list[str] | None
List of DataFrame aliases to be used in sql parameter as duckdb table names.
None














Name
Type
Description





Self
pandas DataFrame enriched by labels + formats + descritption







Usage of obs for selection of observations::

Pass tuple with from-to values for example:obs=(0,4)
or list of from-to tuples, for example:obs=[(1,5),(12,-1),...]
Use (n,None) for reading from n-th row to the last row.


#| echo: true
#| eval: false
    ds=pd.Dataframe(..)
    myDset=ds.ds_options(
        obs=(0,4),
        where="age>14",
        sql="select * from self where sex='F'",
        keep=['Name', 'Age']
        )
